question: [File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the hiero.mirror.restJava.db.statementTimeout (default 10000ms) is a PostgreSQL-side timeout that requires a live database connection to enforce, meaning that during a network partition where the TCP connection is silently dropped, the statement timeout never fires and the JDBC call blocks indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a client-side JDBC socket timeout in the HikariCP configuration for the REST-Java service during a network partition, where the PostgreSQL driver's socketTimeout is not set, causing topicRepository.findById() to block indefinitely on a dead TCP connection?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a memory leak during a network partition by causing many concurrent requests to GET /api/v1/topics/{id} to accumulate blocked threads, each holding references to EntityId objects, Hibernate session state, and JDBC connection wrappers, eventually causing heap exhaustion and a JVM crash?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TopicRepository extends CrudRepository (Spring Data JPA) and that JPA's findById() internally opens a Hibernate session and transaction during a network partition, causing the Hibernate session to remain open and hold a connection even after the request times out at the HTTP layer, resulting in connection leaks?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the circuit breaker (NetworkErrorRatio() > 0.10) requires 10% of requests to fail before opening, meaning an attacker can send exactly 9% error-inducing requests during a partition to stay below the threshold and maintain a persistent low-level denial of service without triggering the circuit breaker?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the retry policy (3 attempts, 100ms interval) at the ingress layer during a network partition to cause a thundering herd effect when the partition heals, where all retried requests simultaneously hit the database, overwhelming the connection pool and causing a secondary denial of service immediately after recovery?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that GenericControllerAdvice.defaultExceptionHandler() logs all unhandled exceptions at ERROR level during a network partition, causing a flood of JDBC connection error logs that overwhelm the logging infrastructure, consuming disk I/O and potentially causing log rotation failures that obscure legitimate security events?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a network partition that affects only the write path (primary database) but not the read path (replica), causing topicRepository.findById() to return data from a replica that has not received recent writes, serving stale topic metadata (e.g., outdated admin keys or submit keys) to clients who rely on this data for security decisions?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TopicServiceImpl.findById() throws EntityNotFoundException with the static message 'Topic not found' regardless of whether the failure is due to a missing topic or a network partition, preventing clients from distinguishing between a legitimate 404 and a partition-induced error, leading to incorrect client-side retry behavior?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the HikariCP connection pool has a fixed maximum size (hikaricp_connections_max) during a network partition to cause a connection pool starvation where all connections are held by blocked topicRepository.findById() calls, and new requests queue indefinitely waiting for a connection, effectively creating an unbounded request queue that consumes heap memory?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a network partition between the REST-Java service and the database to cause the HikariCP keepalive mechanism to fail silently, where keepalive queries are sent on dead connections and never receive responses, causing HikariCP to believe connections are healthy while they are actually dead, leading to a burst of failures when topicRepository.findById() is next called?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service has no rate limiting on GET /api/v1/topics/{id} to send a high volume of requests during a network partition, ensuring that when the partition heals, the accumulated backlog of requests simultaneously floods topicRepository.findById() and causes a secondary database overload?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that EntityId.of(long id) uses a static Caffeine cache with expireAfterAccess=60m during a network partition, where cached EntityId objects for valid topic IDs continue to be served from cache while the underlying database is unreachable, masking the partition from the EntityId resolution layer but not from the actual database query in topicRepository.findById()?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TopicServiceImpl is a final class with no interface-level circuit breaking or fallback, meaning that during a network partition there is no way for the service to return a cached or degraded response, and every call to findById() will attempt a live database query that will block or fail?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service's podDisruptionBudget has minAvailable=50% during a network partition, meaning that if the partition causes enough pods to become unhealthy, Kubernetes may evict additional pods to maintain the budget, further reducing capacity and amplifying the denial of service?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the Prometheus alert RestJavaHighDBConnections fires only after 5 minutes of sustained high connection utilization (>75%), meaning that during a network partition an attacker has a 5-minute window to exhaust the connection pool before any automated remediation is triggered?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service's circuit breaker expression also includes ResponseCodeRatio(500, 600, 0, 600) > 0.25, meaning that during a network partition that causes 500 errors from topicRepository.findById(), the circuit breaker will open after 25% of all requests fail, but by that point significant connection pool exhaustion may have already occurred?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TopicController.getTopic() does not wrap its three service calls in a single transaction or timeout boundary during a network partition, meaning that if topicService.findById() succeeds (using a cached connection) but entityService.findById() blocks on a partitioned connection, the request thread is held indefinitely with no way to cancel the in-progress database operation?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service uses Spring Data JPA's CrudRepository.findById() which internally uses Hibernate's Session.find(), and during a network partition Hibernate may attempt to flush the session before querying, causing additional database operations that further block on the partitioned connection?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service has no explicit connection eviction policy for dead connections during a network partition, causing HikariCP to continue handing out connections that were established before the partition and are now dead, leading to a series of failed topicRepository.findById() calls before HikariCP detects and evicts the dead connections?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service's hiero.mirror.restJava.db.statementTimeout is set to 10000ms (10 seconds) but this timeout is enforced by PostgreSQL, not the JDBC driver, meaning that during a network partition where the TCP connection is silently dropped, the statement timeout signal from PostgreSQL never reaches the client, and the JDBC call blocks beyond the 10-second window?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service's CORS configuration allows all origins (allowedOrigins('*')) to send cross-origin requests to GET /api/v1/topics/{id} during a network partition, enabling browser-based distributed denial of service attacks where many browser clients simultaneously flood the endpoint and exhaust the connection pool?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that TopicServiceImpl.findById() calls id.getId() to extract the encoded long before passing it to topicRepository.findById(), and during a network partition the repository call blocks, but the EntityId.getId() call itself succeeds and populates the Caffeine cache, causing the cache to grow with entries for IDs that will never be successfully resolved from the database?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service has no health check endpoint that specifically tests database connectivity for the topic table, meaning that during a network partition the service may report as healthy to the load balancer while topicRepository.findById() is failing, causing the load balancer to continue routing traffic to an unhealthy instance?

[File: 'File Name: rest-java/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/restjava/service/TopicService.java -> Scope: Critical: Network partition caused outside of design parameters'] [Function: findById(EntityId id)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the REST-Java service's Prometheus alert RestJavaErrors fires only after 2 minutes of sustained error rate >5%, meaning that during a network partition an attacker has a 2-minute window to cause widespread failures before any alert is triggered and operators can respond?
